event.title: US-Iran Ceasefire Extension Framework: Negotiations and Sticking Points
event.summary: US and Iranian negotiators reached a tentative framework agreement to extend their ceasefire by 60 days and launch nuclear talks, but the deal remained unsigned as of late May 2026, pending approval from President Trump and Iranian leadership, with both sides exchanging conflicting statements about the agreement's terms and status.

image.caption: United States Department of State building in Washington, D.C. (file photo)

entities.relevance_to_event: As US President, Trump holds final approval authority over the ceasefire extension framework and has publicly stated contradictory terms for any deal, directly influencing whether negotiations succeed.
Serving as Iran's top negotiator, Ghalibaf's statements rejecting guarantees and threatening renewed conflict represent Iran's official negotiating position on the framework agreement.
As US Vice President, Vance publicly characterized the state of negotiations, stating the sides were 'very close' but disagreed on key sticking points including uranium enrichment.
As US Secretary of State, Rubio met with Pakistan's Foreign Minister Mohammad Ishaq Dar regarding the mediation efforts, positioning him in the diplomatic chain of the negotiation process.
As Pakistan's Foreign Minister, Dar served as the official mediator between Tehran and Washington in these ceasefire extension negotiations.
Iran is one of two principal parties to the ceasefire extension framework, with its leadership and officials making conflicting claims about the agreement's terms and readiness to sign.
The United States is one of two principal parties to the ceasefire extension framework, with its president and officials disputing Iran's characterization of proposed terms and conditions.
Control and governance of the Strait of Hormuz is a core sticking point in the negotiations, with US and Iranian officials making contradictory demands about tolls, access, and mine removal.

claims.text: US and Iranian negotiators agreed on a tentative framework (memorandum of understanding) to extend the ceasefire by 60 days.
claims.rationale: Multiple outlets across the political spectrum — center (BBC, Reuters, Euronews) and center-left (Haaretz, The Guardian) and right (Israel Hayom) — all reported that negotiators had agreed on a tentative 60-day ceasefire extension framework, satisfying the cross-spectrum agreement threshold.

framing_variants.characterization: US officials told BBC the two countries had agreed a framework of a deal known as a memorandum of understanding
Reuters reports Iran and US reached a deal to extend ceasefire, pending Trump's approval
Euronews reports negotiators from both sides reached agreement on a 60-day deal to extend the ceasefire
BBC reports negotiators from both US and Iran sides agreed a framework for a 60-day extension
Euronews reports US and Iranian negotiators reached a tentative framework agreement on Thursday
Guardian describes a reported MOU that would extend the current ceasefire for 60 days
Guardian reports Trump claimed he could approve an Iran peace deal on Friday

claims.text: The ceasefire extension framework required approval from both President Trump and Iranian leadership before taking effect.
claims.rationale: Center outlets (BBC, Reuters, Euronews) and right-leaning outlets (Israel Hayom, Arutz Sheva) all confirmed that the framework awaited Trump's and Iranian leadership's approval, providing cross-spectrum corroboration.

framing_variants.characterization: Reuters notes deal is pending Trump's approval
Euronews says deal awaits final approval from Trump and Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei
BBC states agreement is yet to be approved by Donald Trump and Iranian leadership
Arutz Sheva reports Treasury Secretary said Iran deal depends on what Trump wants to do

claims.text: Trump met with senior advisers for approximately two hours in the White House Situation Room on Friday but concluded the meeting without making a decision on the Iran deal.
claims.rationale: Center-left (The Guardian, Haaretz) and center (Euronews) outlets all reported that Trump's Situation Room meeting ended without a decision, providing cross-spectrum confirmation.

framing_variants.characterization: Trump spent more than two hours in the Situation Room with senior aides; NYT cited senior official saying Trump had not made a decision
Euronews reports meeting concluded without a decision on a potential deal with Iran
Haaretz reports Trump did not reach a decision on an Iran deal

claims.text: US Vice President JD Vance stated that the US and Iran were 'very close' to a deal but still needed to work out 'several sticking points,' including 'the question of enrichment,' and that negotiators were 'going back and forth on a couple of language points.'
claims.rationale: Center (BBC, Euronews) and center-left (The Guardian) outlets all reported Vance's direct statements about the near-but-not-final state of negotiations, spanning the spectrum sufficiently for 'agreed' classification.

framing_variants.characterization: BBC quotes Vance directly: 'We're not there yet, but we're very close and we're going to keep on working at it'; identified enrichment as a sticking point
Euronews quotes Vance saying talks were making 'good progress'
Guardian reports Vance hinted on Thursday night that an agreement was close

claims.text: The US characterized the Iran-reported unofficial draft 14-point memorandum of understanding — which included lifting Washington's naval blockade, US force withdrawal from the 'vicinity of Iran,' and Iran-Oman control of the Strait of Hormuz — as a 'complete fabrication'; Iran's Tasnim news agency reported that the final text had not been completed or approved.
claims.rationale: The White House flatly denied the Iranian state media-reported draft as a 'complete fabrication,' while Iranian sources neither confirmed nor denied, saying the text was not finalized; this is a direct actor dispute about the content and authenticity of a circulating document.

framing_variants.characterization: BBC reports Iranian state media published the draft; White House called it a 'complete fabrication'; Tasnim said it had not been finalised
BBC reports the draft included blockade lifting and Strait management by Iran/Oman but notably lacked Iranian nuclear concessions; White House called it a 'complete fabrication'
Reuters reports Iran state TV said a draft deal would reopen Hormuz shipping and end the naval blockade
Euronews notes IRGC-affiliated Tasnim quoted a source saying the final text had not been completed or approved
Guardian reports Iran's Fars news agency said Trump published a 'mixture of truth and lies' about the terms

claims.text: The reported ceasefire extension framework included: unrestricted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz; Iran removing mines from the strait within 30 days; a proportional lifting of the US naval blockade; US sanctions waivers allowing Iran to sell oil; and Iran's commitment not to pursue nuclear weapons.
claims.rationale: Multiple center and center-left outlets reported these as the terms of the framework, but the articles are predominantly from the same side of the spectrum (center/center-left), and Iran contested some terms, so this is corroborated rather than agreed.

framing_variants.characterization: BBC lists these as the reported framework terms, including 'unrestricted' passage and 30 days for mine removal
Euronews lists same terms as 'draft memorandum' provisions
Guardian attributes these terms to Trump's own public claims about the deal
Guardian describes the reported MOU terms in a background context piece

claims.text: The 60-day ceasefire extension was intended to allow US and Iranian teams to begin nuclear negotiations during the window.
claims.rationale: Center outlets (BBC, Euronews) and center-left (The Guardian) all reported this as the intended purpose of the ceasefire extension, providing cross-spectrum confirmation.

framing_variants.characterization: BBC: 'Extending the ceasefire would allow US and Iranian teams to discuss the far more complicated and technical issues at play, particularly about Iran's nuclear programme'
Euronews reports nuclear negotiations would begin during the 60-day window, with Iran's enriched uranium stockpile as the first agenda item
Guardian states negotiations would take place on the two-decades-old dispute over Iran's nuclear program during the 60-day period

claims.text: Trump publicly stated that any deal would require Iran to never have a nuclear weapon or bomb, the Strait of Hormuz to be immediately opened without tolls, all water mines to be terminated, and that he would lift the US naval blockade; he also claimed he could approve a deal on Friday.
claims.rationale: Center-left (The Guardian) and center (Euronews, Reuters, BBC) outlets all reported Trump's stated deal conditions, providing cross-spectrum corroboration.

framing_variants.characterization: Guardian quotes Trump saying Iran 'must agree that they will never have a Nuclear Weapon or Bomb' and that he could approve a deal on Friday
Euronews quotes Trump's stated red lines on nuclear weapons, Hormuz access without tolls, and mine removal
Reuters reports Trump said he will soon decide on Iran deal and demanded reopening of the Hormuz Strait
BBC notes Trump suggested the US could take Iran's uranium stockpile or dilute it in place or at a third location

claims.text: US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent declined to confirm that an agreement had been reached, saying 'it's always a mistake to get out ahead of the president' and that 'it is all going to be the president's decision.'
claims.rationale: Both BBC (center) and Arutz Sheva (right) reported Bessent's non-confirmation, but these two outlets are not sufficiently separated on the political spectrum to qualify as 'agreed'; they are center and right, which does span the spectrum — however only two sources and both attributed to the same official statement.

framing_variants.characterization: BBC quotes Bessent directly declining to confirm the agreement and deferring to Trump's decision
Arutz Sheva characterizes the story as Treasury Secretary saying the Iran deal depends on what Trump wants to do

claims.text: Iran's foreign ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baqaei stated that exchanges of messages were continuing but no final agreement had been reached, while Iran's Tasnim news agency characterized Trump's announcement as 'in line with his usual pattern of making unilateral and egotistical statements.'
claims.rationale: Center-left (The Guardian) and center (Euronews, BBC) outlets all reported the Iranian foreign ministry's denial of a finalized agreement, spanning the spectrum for 'agreed' classification.

framing_variants.characterization: Guardian quotes Baqaei: 'exchanges of messages are continuing, but no final agreement has been reached yet'; Tasnim called Trump's post egotistical
Euronews quotes Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesperson stating no deal had yet been finalized and that negotiators were focused on ending the war, not nuclear specifics
BBC reports Iran's Tasnim said the framework had 'not been finalised or confirmed'
Euronews notes the IRGC-affiliated Tasnim quoted a source saying the final text had not been completed or approved

claims.text: Trump characterized the deal as requiring Iran to allow the US to 'unearth and destroy highly enriched uranium from a secure nuclear site in Iran'; Iranian officials — including parliamentary speaker Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf and parliament commission chair Ebrahim Azizi — denied any plan to export or transfer enriched uranium, with Azizi stating the Islamic Republic has 'no intention of handing over or transferring these materials.'
claims.rationale: Trump publicly stated the deal would allow US access to destroy Iran's enriched uranium, while multiple Iranian officials explicitly denied any plan to transfer or export their uranium stockpile — a direct actor dispute about a central deal term.

framing_variants.characterization: Guardian reports Trump claimed the deal would allow the US to 'unearth and destroy highly enriched uranium'; Iranian officials repeated there was no plan to allow export of uranium; Fars said Trump published 'a mixture of truth and lies'
BBC outlines US demand that Iran stop producing and dispose of its HEU stockpile; notes Iran insists its programme is peaceful
Euronews reports Iranian parliamentary official Azizi reiterated Iran has no intention of removing enriched uranium from the country
Guardian notes Trump previously said Iran's uranium must be transferred to the US for destruction or destroyed elsewhere; Iranian parliament speakers denied any transfer
Euronews notes Tehran has repeatedly pushed back against nuclear requirements, maintaining nuclear activities are for peaceful and civilian purposes

claims.text: Trump characterized the deal as requiring the Strait of Hormuz to be opened without tolls; Iranian officials — including parliament commission chair Ebrahim Azizi and spokesperson Ebrahim Rezaei — insisted Iran's 'smart management' of the strait was permanent and that Iran would never relinquish control over it.
claims.rationale: Trump demanded toll-free unrestricted passage while Iranian officials explicitly stated Iran's control over the Strait of Hormuz was permanent and non-negotiable — a central actor dispute over the Strait's governance terms.

framing_variants.characterization: Guardian reports Trump demanded 'open the strait of Hormuz for all traffic without tolls'; Rezaei stated Iran was 'in no mood to back down on its plans to change the management of the strait'
Euronews notes Mojtaba Khamenei stated the Islamic Republic will never give up on control over the Strait of Hormuz; Azizi said the policy is permanent
BBC reports US Treasury sanctioned Iran's 'Persian Gulf Strait Authority,' calling it an attempt to 'monetise its campaign of state-sponsored terror'
Reuters notes Trump denied a deal on the Strait of Hormuz
Euronews quotes Trump stating the Hormuz Strait must be immediately open with no tolls

claims.text: Both Iran and the US accused each other of violating the ceasefire, with the IRGC stating it targeted a US base in the region, US Central Command reporting strikes on a 'ground control site' in Bandar Abbas, and the US saying it shot down five Iranian drones.
claims.rationale: Center outlets (BBC, Reuters, Euronews) and center-left (The Guardian, via Euronews framing) all reported mutual ceasefire violation accusations and ongoing military incidents, providing cross-spectrum corroboration.

framing_variants.characterization: BBC: 'both Iran and the US have accused each other of violating the fragile ceasefire'; IRGC said it targeted a US base; fresh US strikes on southern Iran occurred overnight
BBC describes US Centcom strike on 'ground control site' in Bandar Abbas; IRGC said it attacked an American air base; a ballistic missile was intercepted over Kuwait; US shot down five Iranian drones
Euronews notes sporadic exchanges of fire particularly along Iran's southern coast in the Persian Gulf
Euronews: 'Both Iran and US have accused each other of violating the ceasefire'
Reuters reports US struck Iran again, according to an official

claims.text: Iranian state media claimed Iran's forces downed a US aircraft near Bushehr; US Central Command denied the claim, stating 'No US aircraft were shot down' and that 'all US air assets are accounted for.'
claims.rationale: Iranian state media asserted that Iranian forces shot down a US aircraft, while US Central Command flatly denied this — a direct actor dispute about a specific military event.

framing_variants.characterization: BBC reports Iranian state media claimed forces 'taken down a US aircraft, possibly a drone'; US Central Command rejected the reports on social media
Euronews notes Tehran claimed to have downed a US aircraft near Bushehr; CENTCOM denied the claim

claims.text: Pakistan's Foreign Minister Mohammad Ishaq Dar is serving as a mediator between Tehran and Washington; his meeting with US Secretary of State Marco Rubio in Washington ended without any public comment on the negotiations.
claims.rationale: Center (Euronews, BBC) and center-left (The Guardian) outlets all reported Pakistan's mediating role and the inconclusive Rubio-Dar meeting, providing cross-spectrum confirmation.

framing_variants.characterization: Euronews identifies Pakistan's FM Ishaq Dar as mediator and reports his planned Washington meeting with Rubio
Guardian reports the Rubio-Dar meeting ended without any public comment on negotiations
Guardian mentions Pakistani and Qatari mediators involved in negotiations
BBC states the diplomatic effort is 'spearheaded by Pakistan'

claims.text: Iran's parliament speaker Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf, serving as Iran's top negotiator, wrote that Iran had 'no trust in guarantees or words — only actions are the measure' and that 'We seize concessions not through dialogue, but with missiles,' while also stating Iran was ready for another round of fighting if talks failed.
claims.rationale: Center-left (The Guardian) and center (Euronews) outlets all reported Ghalibaf's statements, spanning the spectrum for 'agreed' classification.

framing_variants.characterization: Guardian quotes Ghalibaf: 'no trust in guarantees or words – only actions are the measure'; 'We seize concessions not through dialogue, but with missiles'; ready for another round of fighting
Euronews quotes Ghalibaf: 'We do not gain concessions through talks, but through missiles'; 'No step will be taken before the other side acts'
Euronews notes Ghalibaf was praised in Mojtaba Khamenei's recent message and that the Iranian team is intent on finding a pathway toward ending the war

claims.text: Trump threatened to 'blow up' Oman if it reached any deal with Iran that imposed charges for passage through the Strait of Hormuz.
claims.rationale: Center-left (The Guardian) and center (BBC, Euronews) outlets all reported Trump's explicit threat against Oman, providing cross-spectrum corroboration.

framing_variants.characterization: Guardian quotes Trump: threatened to 'blow up' Oman if it reached any deal with Iran imposing passage charges
BBC quotes Trump directly: 'Oman will behave just like everybody else, or we'll have to blow them up'
Euronews reports Trump directly threatened US regional ally Oman; Iran's Foreign Ministry expressed solidarity with Oman in response

claims.text: Trump is facing mounting domestic pressure to end the war, including from Gulf state allies, Democrats who oppose it, and some Republicans in Congress who have raised concerns about the conflict's length; meanwhile, Iran hawks including Lindsey Graham, Ted Cruz, Roger Wicker, and Mike Pompeo warned against any agreement.
claims.rationale: BBC (center) and The Guardian (center-left) both reported the cross-partisan domestic pressure on Trump, spanning the spectrum for 'agreed' classification.

framing_variants.characterization: BBC: Trump facing pressure from Gulf state allies, Democrats, and some Republicans concerned about conflict length
Guardian emphasizes Republican hawks — Graham, Cruz, Wicker, Pompeo — warning against an agreement, while also noting broader pressure to end war

paragraphs.text: US and Iranian negotiators agreed on a tentative framework to extend their ceasefire by 60 days, according to reports from outlets across the political spectrum. The memorandum of understanding would allow diplomatic teams from both countries to begin nuclear negotiations during the extension window. However, the framework required approval from both President Trump and Iranian leadership before taking effect, and as of late May 2026, no final agreement had been signed.

US Vice President JD Vance stated that the US and Iran were "very close" to a deal but still needed to work out "several sticking points," including "the question of enrichment." Vance added that negotiators were "going back and forth on a couple of language points." Trump met with senior advisers for approximately two hours in the White House Situation Room on Friday but concluded the meeting without making a decision on the Iran deal.

Iran's foreign ministry spokesperson Esmaeil Baqaei stated that exchanges of messages were continuing but no final agreement had been reached. Iran's Tasnim news agency characterized Trump's announcement as "in line with his usual pattern of making unilateral and egotistical statements." US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent declined to confirm that an agreement had been reached, saying "it's always a mistake to get out ahead of the president" and that "it is all going to be the president's decision."

Pakistan's Foreign Minister Mohammad Ishaq Dar served as a mediator between Tehran and Washington. His meeting with US Secretary of State Marco Rubio in Washington ended without any public comment on the negotiations.

Center and center-left outlets reported that the tentative framework included several specific terms: unrestricted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz; Iran removing mines from the strait within 30 days; a proportional lifting of the US naval blockade; US sanctions waivers allowing Iran to sell oil; and Iran's commitment not to pursue nuclear weapons. However, key elements of these reported terms became subject to direct contradictions between US and Iranian officials.

Trump publicly stated that any deal would require Iran to never have a nuclear weapon or bomb, the Strait of Hormuz to be immediately opened without tolls, all water mines to be terminated, and that he would lift the US naval blockade. Trump also claimed he could approve a deal on Friday.

Iran's parliament speaker Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf, serving as Iran's top negotiator, wrote that Iran had "no trust in guarantees or words — only actions are the measure" and that "We seize concessions not through dialogue, but with missiles." Ghalibaf also stated Iran was ready for another round of fighting if talks failed.

Trump threatened to "blow up" Oman if it reached any deal with Iran that imposed charges for passage through the Strait of Hormuz. The threat came amid broader tensions over the strait's governance and access terms.

Trump faced mounting domestic pressure to end the war, including from Gulf state allies, Democrats who opposed it, and some Republicans in Congress who raised concerns about the conflict's length. Meanwhile, Iran hawks including Lindsey Graham, Ted Cruz, Roger Wicker, and Mike Pompeo warned against any agreement.

The US characterized an Iran-reported unofficial draft 14-point memorandum of understanding — which included lifting Washington's naval blockade, US force withdrawal from the "vicinity of Iran," and Iran-Oman control of the Strait of Hormuz — as a "complete fabrication." Iran's Tasnim news agency reported that the final text had not been completed or approved. The White House flatly denied the Iranian state media-reported draft, while Iranian sources neither confirmed nor denied its authenticity, saying only that the text was not finalized.

Trump characterized the deal as requiring Iran to allow the US to "unearth and destroy highly enriched uranium from a secure nuclear site in Iran." Iranian officials — including parliamentary speaker Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf and parliament commission chair Ebrahim Azizi — denied any plan to export or transfer enriched uranium. Azizi stated the Islamic Republic has "no intention of handing over or transferring these materials."

Trump characterized the deal as requiring the Strait of Hormuz to be opened without tolls. Iranian officials — including parliament commission chair Ebrahim Azizi and spokesperson Ebrahim Rezaei — insisted Iran's "smart management" of the strait was permanent and that Iran would never relinquish control over it.

Both Iran and the US accused each other of violating the ceasefire during the negotiation period. The IRGC stated it targeted a US base in the region, while US Central Command reported strikes on a "ground control site" in Bandar Abbas. The US said it shot down five Iranian drones.

Iranian state media claimed Iran's forces downed a US aircraft near Bushehr. US Central Command denied the claim, stating "No US aircraft were shot down" and that "all US air assets are accounted for."
